items.q: Why can a cold winter NOT disprove climate change?
items.correct: Climate is the long-term statistical average; a single cold winter is a weather event. Climate change is the trend in averages and extremes over decades, not the absence of cold days.
items.misconception: A really cold winter proves global warming is not happening.
items.random_wrong: Climate change only happens in summer.
items.why: This is one of the most common public misconceptions. Climate is the slope of the trend over 30+ years; individual weather events are scatter around that line — both can be cold.

items.q: Why is the Brandt Line considered outdated?
items.correct: Drawn in 1980, it predates the rise of NICs (China, India, Brazil) and globalisation — countries like South Korea, Singapore and Chile no longer fit neatly on the 'poor south' side.
items.misconception: The Brandt Line still accurately divides rich and poor countries today.
items.random_wrong: The Brandt Line is the boundary between the Atlantic and Pacific oceans.
items.why: The world has changed: BRICs have grown, some HICs have stagnated. A simple north-south divide cannot capture this. Many geographers prefer the term 'Global North/Global South' loosely or reject the binary altogether.

items.q: What does a 'large scale' map mean?
items.correct: A large-scale map covers a small area in high detail (e.g. 1:25,000 OS map of one town); a small-scale map covers a large area in less detail (e.g. 1:1,000,000 world atlas).
items.misconception: A large-scale map shows a large area.
items.random_wrong: Large scale means the map is physically big.
items.why: This is counter-intuitive but mathematically logical: 1/25,000 is a larger fraction than 1/1,000,000. Students reverse the terminology and consistently mark themselves down on map skill questions.
Correct answer: Richter. “Mercalli” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The San Andreas Fault is what type of margin?
items.why: Correct answer: Conservative. “Constructive” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What was the magnitude of the 2010 Haiti earthquake?
items.why: Exact answer: 7 Richter. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
